draft.title: a CRM for freelancers
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a CRM for freelancers

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer conversational, encouraging copy that feels like a helpful colleague rather than corporate software
facade.label: Casual chat tone
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into warmth, humor, and first-person language across onboarding, notifications, and error states
facade.rejectImplies: Adopt formal, instructional tone; prioritize clarity and authority over rapport

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer a persistent navigation sidebar paired with card-based layouts for client/project information, reducing cognitive load from modal switching
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation+Content Structure — persistent sidebar vs top tabs; card density
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: Explore sidebar-first patterns with varying card grid densities; test 2-3 column layouts
facade.rejectImplies: Pivot toward tab-based or full-width list interfaces; test horizontal scrolling or modal-driven workflows

evidence.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer conversational, encouraging copy that feels like a helpful colleague rather than corporate software
evidence.implication: Lean into warmth, humor, and first-person language across onboarding, notifications, and error states

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers prefer seeing clients as individual interactive cards rather than a compact table row format
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data vs key metrics — whether to show one client per card (sparse, visual) or multiple clients per view (dense, tabular)
facade.label: Client list as cards
facade.acceptImplies: The CRM should prioritize visual breathing room and single-client focus; likely means larger cards, maybe one per screen or scrollable grid with white space
facade.rejectImplies: Freelancers need to scan many clients at once; table or list format is more efficient; the design should show multiple rows per view with high information density

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers respond better to approachable, gentle interface geometry than sharp/corporate edges
facade.axisTargeted: Roundness vs. sharpness in button and card design
facade.label: Soft, rounded corners
facade.acceptImplies: Future probes should test warm, inviting micro-interactions and softer color palettes; consider organic shapes in UI elements
facade.rejectImplies: Explore cleaner, more minimal or angular design systems; test crisp, modern aesthetic with sharp borders and lines

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers respond positively to organic, tactile aesthetics that feel handmade and human—reducing the coldness of data entry and client management.
facade.label: Warm clay forms
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into natural materials, rounded shapes, and crafted textures. The CRM should feel like a physical notebook or workspace, not a sterile database.
facade.rejectImplies: Move toward cleaner, more geometric interfaces. Digital-native aesthetics with sharper lines, lighter color palettes, and system-like precision.

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers need frictionless access to client names—zero loading, no navigation layers—to match how they naturally recall contacts during calls or quick decisions.
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior: instant-access vs load-time, flat-list vs hierarchical navigation
facade.label: Instant contact list
facade.acceptImplies: Users value speed over visual polish; minimal transitions, instant-render interfaces will resonate. CRM should prioritize snappy data retrieval and flat information architecture.
facade.rejectImplies: Users may prefer structured browsing, search-first patterns, or layered discovery over raw speed. Loading states, filtered views, or animated transitions might actually help them organize contacts mentally.

axes.label: Whether they prefer minimal, clean aesthetics vs. rich visual hierarchy with color/imagery—reveals if they want a distraction-free workspace or one that feels energetic and branded
Whether they favor a dashboard-first overview vs. task-list-first workflow—reveals how they mentally organize client work and priorities
Whether they want a professional, formal tone vs. casual, encouraging tone—reveals if they see the tool as a business utility or a supportive partner
Whether they prefer a calm, zen workspace vs. one that feels energizing and motivating—reveals their emotional relationship to admin work
Whether they want all client info on one screen vs. organized into separate focused views—reveals their tolerance for information density and navigation depth
Whether they expect instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, deliberate transitions—reveals if they value speed or clarity in tool responsiveness

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they prefer minimal, clean aesthetics vs. rich visual hierarchy with color/imagery—reveals if they want a distraction-free workspace or one that feels energetic and branded
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they favor a dashboard-first overview vs. task-list-first workflow—reveals how they mentally organize client work and priorities
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they want a professional, formal tone vs. casual, encouraging tone—reveals if they see the tool as a business utility or a supportive partner
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they prefer a calm, zen workspace vs. one that feels energizing and motivating—reveals their emotional relationship to admin work
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they want all client info on one screen vs. organized into separate focused views—reveals their tolerance for information density and navigation depth
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether they expect instant feedback and snappy interactions vs. smooth, deliberate transitions—reveals if they value speed or clarity in tool responsiveness
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Freelancers respond to encouraging, motivational language that celebrates their wins and acknowledges their hustle, making the CRM feel like a supportive partner rather than a task tracker
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — encouraging vs neutral
facade.label: Cheering you on tone
facade.acceptImplies: Weave celebratory language into dashboards, milestone notifications, and success moments (e.g., 'You crushed that deadline!' vs 'Project completed')
facade.rejectImplies: Keep tone factual and minimal — let data speak for itself without emotional scaffolding or cheerleading
